student_message: Oh cool! It is like a spinning windmill! I bet 'Z' is a trick too, since the zig-zags won't match when folded. Can you explain if there is a special math name for shapes that spin but don't fold?
final_message: You are so smart, Zara! You are exactly right about the letter Z being a trick just like the letter S. If you try to fold a Z in half, the zig-zags will not match up at all, but if you spin it halfway around, it looks exactly the same! There is a special math name for this spinning magic, and it is called rotational symmetry. Whenever a shape or a letter can be turned around a center point and still look exactly like it did before you moved it, it has rotational symmetry. Your windmill example is absolutely perfect for this! So while things like a letter A or a pizza slice have reflection symmetry with fold lines, your spinning Z and S have rotational symmetry. You are doing such a brilliant job thinking about these shapes! 🌟

orchestrator_intent.goal: Explain rotational symmetry and validate her observation about the letter Z.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student is engaged and asked a specific conceptual question about shapes that spin but don't fold. The dialogue agent is best suited to explain this.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
